articles.title: Why Doxxing Remains a Legal Grey Area: Navigating the Legal Uncertainty of Online Exposure
articles.summary: This analysis delves into the implications of doxxing in India, the legal frameworks in place, and the steps individuals can take to protect themselves.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In recent years, India has witnessed a spike in doxxing incidents targeting journalists, activists, women, students, and public figures, often as a form of online retaliation or harassment. Doxxing is the intentional public release onto the Internet of personal information about an individual by a third party, often with the intent to humiliate, threaten, intimidate, or punish the identified individual. Background The recent escalation of tensions between India and Pakistan was mirrored on social media, where discourse quickly became polarized and hostile. Individuals expressing even mildly dissenting or peace-oriented views were swiftly targeted. A prominent far-right account, with approximately 1,29,000 followers, escalated the situation by publicly sharing the personal information of those advocating for de-escalation. This included their Instagram handles, LinkedIn profiles, and other publicly available social media accounts, effectively encouraging its followers to harass and intimidate them online. While the world has rapidly embraced the digital age, one of its more troubling consequences is the erosion of personal privacy. Doxxing stands out as a stark example of this intrusion, where individuals' private information is exposed online without consent. Today, it includes publishing details such as home addresses, phone numbers, workplace information, photos, or even financial and medical records. These disclosures frequently result in threats, abuse, and real-world consequences, ranging from job loss to physical harm. While doxxing is a global issue, it has taken on a distinct character in India due to its complex socio-political landscape, rapid digitization, and relatively underdeveloped legal protections for personal privacy online. Despite its serious implications, India’s legal framework offers limited recourse for victims of doxxing. It presents significant challenges to privacy, freedom of expression, and safety. Therefore, there is an urgent need for robust legal protections, awareness campaigns, and institutional capacity-building to counter this growing threat. Why should you care? Doxxing is a form of digital violence that exposes people to real-world harm. It involves publishing private information like phone numbers, addresses, Aadhaar details, or photos without consent, often to intimidate or silence. It has been used against protesting students, journalists , civil servants , activists, and especially women which ultimately leads to harassment, abuse, and threats of violence. Recently, India's Foreign Secretary, Vikram Misri, became the target of online abuse after merely announcing that India and Pakistan had agreed to cease military operations. Social media trolls inundated him with offensive remarks, and disturbingly, his daughter was also drawn into the controversy. Her phone number was publicly shared online. Despite the growing number of cases, there is currently no specific legislation that addresses it directly. Victims are left to navigate a fragmented legal framework, relying on select provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000 (‘IT Act’) and the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (‘BNS’), none of which comprehensively address the unique nature of this harm. In a country where digital platforms increasingly mediate public and private life, the misuse of personal data can cause swift and irreparable damage. While the need for a dedicated anti-doxxing law remains urgent, some protection and redress is still possible under the existing statutes. What does the law say about doxxing? The term "doxxing" finds its origins in the slang expression "dropping dox"—a practice that emerged from 1990s hacker subculture, where individuals sought revenge by uncovering and publishing the real identities of those who had deliberately chosen to remain anonymous online. Over time, this act of forced exposure evolved into a broader digital harm, now widely recognized as a violation of informational privacy. One major incident of doxxing in India was in the Bulli Bai and Sulli Deals Cases . In July, 2021, an app titled “Sulli Deals” on GitHub was used to ‘auction’ Muslim women. Similarly, an app on GitHub titled “Bulli Bai” put up for ‘auction’ around 100 Muslim women. The platforms used doctored images and sourced the personal information of the victims from their social media accounts. The Delhi Police arrested individuals involved in these cases, and the National Commission for Women took suo motu cognizance. The Internet Freedom Foundation (IFF) extended support to several victims and wrote to the Delhi Police to expeditiously investigate these cases. IFF also submitted its recommendations to the National Commission for Women (NCW) and the Delhi Commission for Women (DCW). Under Indian law, though “doxxing” does not exist as a standalone offence, the IT Act and the BNS offer placeholder safeguards against the malicious or unauthorised publication of personal information, particularly when such disclosure is intended to harass, intimidate, or shame the victim. Section 66E criminalises capturing or sharing images of someone’s private parts without their consent, especially in situations where the person had a reasonable expectation of privacy. Similarly, Sections 67 , 67A , and 67B cover the electronic transmission of obscene, sexually explicit, or child sexual abuse content—offences that often overlap with gender-based doxxing. Section 72 also plays an important role, especially when private information is leaked by someone who had lawful access to it, like a platform moderator, system administrator, or employee. This becomes particularly relevant in cases of internal data leaks or security lapses that lead to mass doxxing. In State of West Bengal v. Animesh Boxi (2018), the accused posted photographs of the victim, along with her name and her father’s name, on a pornographic website without her permission. He was convicted under Section 66E and Section 67 of the IT Act 2000 along with sections of the Indian Penal Code. While the judgment did not explicitly reference the term doxxing, the court acknowledged the gravity of such online privacy violations and set an important legal precedent for addressing digital offenses of this nature. The BNS complements these digital protections with provisions that target behaviour commonly associated with or resulting from doxxing. Section 78 criminalises stalking, including persistent online tracking or contact, while Section 79 addresses privacy violations against women, including unauthorised surveillance and the public dissemination of personal details. Section 356 on defamation may be invoked when doxxing is used to smear someone’s reputation, and Section 294 could apply when the published content is vulgar or meant to humiliate. The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (“DPDP Act”) introduces a more general rights-based framework for data protection, requiring explicit consent for the collection, processing, and sharing of personal data. However, its effectiveness in dealing with doxxing is limited. The Act doesn’t apply to personal data that is “made or caused to be made publicly available”—a vague carveout that creates uncertainty in situations where someone’s information is shared online without consent, but is claimed to be publicly accessible. This means if someone’s information is shared online and claimed to already be public, it’s unclear if the law can protect that person. Because of this, victims of doxxing may not always have legal protection. Evidently, these provisions may serve as placeholders but they do not sufficiently address the variety of consequences that can follow a case of doxxing. This gap was acknowledged by the Orissa High Court in Subhranshu Rout @ Gugul v. State of Odisha (2020), where photos and videos of a victim of rape were uploaded without her consent. In its judgement, the Court acknowledged its limited ability to provide effective redress due to the absence of specific provisions such as a "right to be forgotten" or mechanisms for permanent removal of such content from social media platforms. According to the DPDP Act, 2023, individuals are empowered with the "right to erasure," enabling them to request the deletion of their personal data under specific conditions. However, the DPDP Act doesn't grant individuals the explicit right to have their data removed from online sources when it is outdated or irrelevant, like the General Data Protection Regulation ("GDPR"), a key aspect of the right to be forgotten. Shaviya Sharma and the Reasonable Expectation of Privacy This brings us to the case of Shaviya Sharma v. Squint Neon (“ Shaviya Sharma ”), where the plaintiff’s tweet about a “public political figure” triggered an outrage on social media, during which her personal and professional details including photographs and employer information were widely circulated. The Delhi High Court, while granting relief and directing X Corp. to release the Basic Subscriber Information of the offending accounts, held that the case did not constitute doxxing. It held that the information that had been shared by the accounts was not ‘private’ in the legal sense, as the plaintiff’s tweet included her initials and profile picture, making her easily identifiable to those familiar with her. Shaviya Sharma is one of the first cases in the Indian higher judiciary to discuss and adjudicate on a doxxing claim, and for that it is an important touchstone. However, its emphasis on the initial ‘anonymity’ of the information sets up a dangerous defence for future cases, and misunderstands the idea of doxxing. Doxxing is not just the revealing of personal information that is previously unknown. It is the sharing of someone’s personal information by a third party without their consent. The legal assumption in Shaviya Sharma is that once you have shared certain personal information with a third-party, you can no longer claim that information to be anonymous or ‘private’. However, this has been explicitly rejected by the Supreme Court in District Collector, Hyderabad v. Canara Bank, (2005) 1 SCC 496, where a two-judge bench of the Supreme Court held that privacy attaches to persons and not places, meaning that the reasonable expectation of privacy over personal information survives even if one chooses to share it with a third party. In the digital age, dangerous personal information such as one’s employer, contact details and likeness can be gathered through basic searches with minimal effort. However, the easy accessibility of that information does not necessarily render it ‘public’ information. Your photograph and name being displayed via a social media platform does not (a) take away one’s reasonable expectation of privacy over that information, and (b) does not give others a right to use that information without your consent. What to do if you are Doxxed? If you find yourself a victim of doxxing in India, it's crucial to take immediate and systematic action to protect your safety and seek redress. Here's a step-by-step guide: Document the Incident : Take screenshots of the offensive content, including URLs, timestamps, and any identifying information. This evidence is vital for both reporting and potential legal proceedings. Report to Platforms : Utilise the reporting mechanisms provided by social media platforms like Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), and Google to flag the content. These platforms are obligated under India's IT Rules to act on such complaints. File a Cybercrime Complaint : Visit the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal to file a complaint. Additionally, you can approach your local cybercrime cell or police station to lodge a First Information Report (FIR). If the local station is uncooperative, you have the right to file a Zero FIR, which mandates that the police record the complaint regardless of jurisdiction. Seek Legal Recourse : While India lacks a specific law against doxxing, various provisions under the BNS and the Information Technology Act, 2000, can be invoked. These include sections on defamation (Section 356 BNS), online harassment (Section 78 BNS), and breach of privacy (Section 66E of IT Act). Consulting with a legal professional can help determine the best course of action based on the specifics of your case. Conclusion Doxxing poses a multi-faceted challenge as an act of digital aggression. While some courts have dealt with doxxing manifesting as gendered online violence, instances of doxxing carried out against activists and journalists still remain outside the current legal framework. Recently, on November 8, 2024, journalist Rana Ayyub reported being doxxed when her personal contact information was shared online, leading to harassment and threats. Doxxing in this context is inherently political, intended to produce a chilling effect to stifle the speech of dissenting individuals. Significantly, in the face of rising instances of doxxing against activists, politicians and journalists, countries such as Germany and the Netherlands have adopted definitions that focus on the perpetrator’s intention to harm instead of the private/public nature of the information. These countries’ criminal codes penalise the public dissemination of any personal information of another person when intended to put that person or a person close to them in danger or to frighten/intimidate them. Legislative adoption of such a definition in India would move beyond the limits imposed by the Delhi High Court in Shaviya Sharma and would provide better, more universal protection to victims of doxxing. Important Documents Shaviya Sharma vs. Squint Neon & Ors., CS(OS) 134 of 2024 ( Link ) Information Technology Act, 2000 ( Link ) Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 ( Link )

articles.title: A RTI based investigation of Misinformation Control and Fact-Check Units
articles.summary: This post is the Part 1 of our investigation around fact check unites based on RTIs filed with states and Union Territories. The responses reveal distinct models of fact checking that give rise to concerns for free expression.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr State governments are setting up, or claiming to have constituted fact-check units to tackle online misinformation. Through Right to Information requests to all States and Union Territories starting in July, 2024 we sought details on their measures. The responses reveal concerns including — vague procedures, risk of political bias, and lack of independent oversight — raising the threat of arbitrary censorship and erosion of free speech. In this blogpost, which forms the first part of a three part series, we will analyse one of the most common responses we received – criminalisation of spreading misinformation and subsequent involvement of law enforcement agencies. Why should you care? The way misinformation is tackled directly affects everyone as more people rely on online platforms to keep updated. Here, both misinformation and the censorship of speech impacts our fundamental rights to receive information and in turn free speech. When governments and law enforcement agencies are given broad, unchecked powers to monitor and remove online content without clear safeguards, it opens the door to arbitrary censorship, political bias, and suppression of dissent. Hence, without strong protections, systems meant to fight misinformation can easily be misused to silence criticism, stifle public debate, and weaken democratic accountability. Protecting freedom of expression, ensuring transparency in governance, and demanding fair and independent mechanisms are essential not just for activists or journalists, but for every citizen who values an open and democratic society. Background After the brutal terrorist attack in Pahalgam on April 22, 2025 we have witnessed rampant misinformation spike online. Social media platforms have become battlegrounds where false narratives, doctored videos, and unverified claims are spreading at an alarming rate, often faster than official updates can keep up. Fake footage of military strikes, misattributed images of destruction, and fabricated government announcements are being widely circulated to inflame public sentiment, rally nationalist fervour, or discredit the opposing side. At the same time there has been illegal censorship which has been implemented in violation of any principles of natural justice. In this context, it becomes especially important to understand how governments are currently responding to misinformation. At this point many have stated the need for the government to fact check more proactively. However, an expansion of any censorial power which accompanies it has already been attempted and struck down as unconstitutional. A short refresher on it may be useful at this point. The Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Amendment Rules, 2023 (" IT Rules, 2023 ") empowered the Central Government to establish a Fact-Check Unit (“ FCU ”) with broad powers to evaluate online content and enforce compliance. The terms "fake," "false," or "misleading" information, along with the vague concept of the "business of the Central Government," granted the FCU extensive discretion in determining what constitutes misinformation about government affairs. On September 26, 2024, in a landmark ruling , the Bombay High Court struck down this provision, declaring it unconstitutional (read more here ). Justice Chandurkar (the third judge), in his judgement , disagreed with the Central Government's argument that the right to freedom of speech under Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution included a "right to the truth," which would require the State to ensure the dissemination of only accurate information. He found that the IT Rules, 2023 violated Article 19(2) by imposing restrictions beyond its permissible scope, arbitrarily distinguished between digital and print media (violating Articles 19(1)(g) and 14), and vested unchecked power in the government without clear standards, creating a chilling effect on free speech. Furthermore, the IT Rules, 2023, introduced substantive obligations that extended beyond the mandate of the Information Technology Act (“ IT Act ”), 2000, especially Sections 69A and 79, and lacked necessary safeguards against misuse. Despite this ruling, numerous state governments have moved ahead, setting up their own FCUs (or similar bodies) to purportedly address misinformation online. A RTI based study of state level FCUs To better understand these evolving state-level frameworks, we filed two sets of Right to Information (“ RTI ”) requests with the Home Departments of all states and Union Territories in July 2024, followed by two additional sets in January 2025. The first (generic) set of RTIs focused on measures adopted to monitor and control fake, false, and misleading information, while the second (specific) sought detailed information regarding the legal authority, structure, composition, powers, and standard operating procedures of any established FCUs or similar bodies. It is important to note that we were unable to file RTIs with Rajasthan due to procedural barriers, including the mandatory requirement of an Aadhaar ID and a Google account to access their online RTI portal. The responses were systematically reviewed and analysed to assess the operational frameworks and practices of each state. A clear pattern emerged: either there was the involvement of law enforcement agencies and the criminalisation of misinformation, or the constitution of FCUs, which may also lead to criminalisation, alongside states citing confidentiality and claiming exemption under the RTI Act. Kerala 09.01.2025 Link 15.01.2025 Link 18.07.2025 Returned 23.07.2024 Returned This is the first in a three-part series on how states are tackling online misinformation. This first part explores the role of law enforcement agencies in addressing misinformation, the second part will delve into the constitution of FCUs, and the third part will examine states that have claimed exemptions under the RTI Act. Reliance on Law Enforcement Agencies Across Indian states, law enforcement agencies are playing an increasingly central role in identifying and responding to online misinformation, often through the criminalisation of spreading false information under criminal laws and the IT Act, 2000. While this reflects a heightened concern over the harms of misleading information, the strategies adopted by states differ widely in terms of scope, intensity, legal grounding, and transparency. A review of state-level RTI responses reveals significant variation in how law enforcement agencies monitor, act upon, and regulate misinformation, highlighting the diverse approaches employed across the country. 1. Monitoring Mechanisms: Proactive Surveillance versus Complaint-Based Action Several states have established proactive mechanisms to monitor misinformation. In Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram , cyber crime units actively scan social media platforms. Similarly, Maharashtra and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands (Cyber Police) assign their cyber police teams to conduct real-time monitoring and intelligence gathering around potentially harmful or misleading content. Different districts in Jammu and Kashmir operate either Social Media Monitoring Cells, Electronic Surveillance Units (“ ESU ”), or Cyber Crime Cells, reflecting a more institutionalised surveillance structure. Conversely, in states like Karnataka and Tripura, misinformation monitoring is largely reactive. In a few districts of Karnataka ( Kempegowda , VV Puram , Shankarapura , CKA ) district police teams are tasked with this role. While in other districts ( Girinagar and KS Layout ), the aggrieved person may approach the police station and appropriate action under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (“ BNS ”) and IT Act, 2000 will be taken as per the law. If the news is disturbing public order then suo-moto action may be taken. In Tripura , while social media is monitored, some districts act only upon receipt of public complaints. Chandigarh (responses from DSP Cyber Crime to specific RTI, 2024 and DSP Cyber Crime to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) follows a similar approach, where complaints submitted to the Social Media Team may lead to action, but only after legal vetting and approval by competent authorities. 2. Takedown Practices and Legal Authority States vary considerably in their capacity and authority to act on content identified as harmful. Punjab (responses from Cyber Crime Cell to generic RTI, 2024 and Cyber Crime Cell to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) invokes Sections 69A and 79(3)(b) of the Information Technology Act, 2000, to initiate takedowns or legal proceedings. Maharashtra (Cyber Police) also invokes these provisions, in conjunction with the BNS, 2023, and coordinates with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (“ MeitY ”) and CERT-In for content removal under the Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (“ IT Rules, 2021 ”). In Delhi , the police are designated as the nodal agency under Section 79(3)(b) of the IT Act, 2000, as outlined in the IT Rules, 2021. Content removal is overseen by the Joint Commissioner of Police. However, as with other states, there are no publicly available criteria outlining what constitutes "harmful" or "misleading" content, raising concerns about subjectivity in enforcement. Copy of Delhi Gazette dated December 26, 2024 The Andaman and Nicobar Islands represent a more extreme case. Here, the CID not only removes and prosecutes content under Section 79(3)(b), but they also resort to internet shutdowns in cases where misinformation is perceived to incite violence or disorder. While individuals may seek judicial redress if their content is removed, the discretionary power exercised by authorities remains largely unchecked. Law enforcement in these islands also collaborates closely with internet service providers, social media companies, and central cyber crime units for monitoring and enforcement. Screenshot of response from Superintendent of Police, CID, Andaman and Nicobar dated September 14, 2024 to the generic RTI filed in 2024 Chandigarh (responses from DSP Cyber Crime to specific RTI, 2024 and DSP Cyber Crime to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) requires written complaints before initiating takedown procedures, and only proceed once a competent authority grants permission. Interestingly, in Arunachal Pradesh , where 55 social media profiles were removed by the cyber crime unit via the Law Enforcement Online Request System (“ LEORS ”), authorities refused to disclose the rationale or case details, citing exemptions under Section 8(1)(h) of the RTI Act, 2005. This lack of transparency impairs public trust and impedes oversight. Similarly, one of the districts in Haryana – Faridabad claims to issue takedown notices for fake or misleading content but maintains no official records of these actions. Our Concerns The increasing reliance on law enforcement agencies and the criminalisation of misinformation raise significant concerns about the balance between safeguarding public order and protecting citizens' rights to free speech. While curbing harmful content is a legitimate concern, the lack of clear, transparent guidelines for identifying and managing misinformation leaves room for potential abuse of power. The broad discretion granted to law enforcement—whether at the district level in Karnataka or through Delhi Police—raises the risk of arbitrary enforcement. This could lead to selective action, where certain viewpoints or groups are disproportionately targeted, especially when definitions of "fake" or "misleading" content are vague and open to interpretation. States like Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram, where cyber crime units actively monitor social media, and Tripura, which relies on public complaints, exemplify how the lack of specific criteria can lead to biased or politically motivated action, suppressing dissenting voices or minority opinions. In more extreme cases, such as the internet shutdowns in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, law enforcement overreach in the name of combating misinformation disrupts citizens' access to information and stifles public discourse. This limits the free flow of ideas, further compounding risks to civil liberties. The threats posed by criminal prosecutions The responses to our RTIs also reflect the growing use of the BNS, 2023, as a formalised tool for criminalising digital speech. Provisions such as Section 353, which targets the circulation of false information likely to cause public alarm, and Section 197(1)(d), which addresses actions prejudicial to national integration, have been invoked by several states alongside the IT Act, 2000. These provisions prescribe significant penalties, underscoring the shift towards punitive state responses. The vague language in these laws—such as references to "public tranquillity" or "prejudice to national integrity"—further invites subjective interpretation and potential misuse. Section 69A of the IT Act, 2000, which empowers the government to block online content, also ties non-compliance with blocking orders to criminal penalties, including imprisonment of up to seven years. In this context, the Madras High Court's judgment in A. Kamala v. The State and Others offers a crucial counterpoint to the criminalisation trend. Responding to the detention of an individual under the Tamil Nadu Goondas Act, 1982, for allegedly circulating false information about a tender process, the Court asserted that not all instances of misinformation justify criminal prosecution—especially when there is no immediate threat to public order. The Court further underscored the need for preventive detention to be grounded in clear, tangible evidence, warning against the misuse of "public order" as a justification for suppressing dissent. The Court's emphasis on protecting social media expressions, even if critical or unpleasant, highlights the importance of safeguarding free speech in a climate where misinformation laws are applied inconsistently and arbitrarily. Unfortunately, governments across India are increasingly turning to laws like the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (“ UAPA ”), 1967, and sedition provisions to criminalise misinformation, particularly when it is critical of the state (see here and here ). These laws, originally designed to address genuine threats to national security, have been extended to target online speech, where the broad and often vague definitions of “unlawful activities” and “sedition” can be leveraged to suppress dissent. This trend is troubling, as it risks misusing these provisions to stifle legitimate political discourse, further eroding protections for free expression in the digital era. Conclusion The state-level responses to misinformation reveal key concerns around freedom of speech. The growing role of law enforcement and criminalisation of misinformation, combined with the absence of clear, standardised criteria, raises the risk of arbitrary enforcement and censorship. Without transparent guidelines, legitimate dissent may be wrongly targeted. The lack of uniformity across states—some using police, others cyber units or complaint-based systems—leads to inconsistent treatment of citizens based on geography. This fragmented approach heightens the risk of biased regulation and misuse of power. A more transparent, standardised, and legally grounded framework is urgently needed. Clear definitions, independent oversight, and safeguards must be put in place to ensure efforts to combat misinformation do not compromise democratic freedoms.

articles.title: 'I feel betrayed': US Marine veteran after father assaulted, arrested by masked agents
articles.summary: A US Marine veteran expressed feeling betrayed after his father, Narciso Barranco, was allegedly assaulted and arrested during an ICE workplace raid in Santa Ana, California. The incident, captured on video, shows masked federal agents, identified as US Border Patrol Police, pinning Narciso to the ground.

articles.title: 'Over 1,000 Iranians arrived during Biden's term': Pam Bondi amid US-Iran tensions
articles.summary: Attorney General Pam Bondi revealed that over 1,000 Iranian citizens entered the U.S. during the Biden administration, raising concerns amidst heightened U.S.-Iran tensions. Border Patrol agents apprehended over 1,500 Iranians at the southern border, with nearly half being released into the country. Bondi declined to comment on potential Iranian "sleeper cells" due to the sensitive nature of the topic.

articles.title: India Blocks Satirical Page ‘Savala Vada’ On Instagram, Raising Free Speech Concerns
articles.summary: The Indian Govt has instructed Instagram to block 'Savala Vada', a satirical news page, amid rising free speech concerns in the country. The post India Blocks Satirical Page ‘Savala Vada’ On Instagram, Raising Free Speech Concerns appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: Satirical news Instagram page The Savala Vada is no longer accessible in India as of June 23. Instagram says that it received a legal request to block Savala Vada’s Instagram page in India, and upon reviewing the request against its own policies, it restricted the content because it goes against local laws. In a statement about the situation (which is inaccessible in India) the Savala Vada mentions that while it anticipated such an action, the timing and reach of such an action seems ‘ridiculous’. The page asks the Government to inform it which joke it didn’t like, further joking that in taking down the page, the Government has “vanquished Public Enemy #1 : A meme page run out of a hostel dorm.” Government action comes after the page previously posted satirical commentary stating that India would ban Turkish ice cream parlours across Indian malls the next time Turkey helps out Pakistan. The joke came in the context of Turkey helping Pakistan militarily during its armed conflict against India last month. In another post, the page satirically commented on the case involving an Ashoka University professor who was imprisoned for commenting on Operation Sindoor. “In an attempt to encourage people to post more about Bharat’s Greatest Feminist War Operation, Ashoka University professor Ali Khan Mahmudabad has been provided with free government-sponsored accommodation and food after being sent to 14-day judicial custody for his posts,” the page mentioned. The rapid rise of social media takedowns in India: The Government’s action against Savala Vada comes in line with the numerous content takedowns that the Centre has ordered since the Pahalgam terror attack in April this year. Between April and May 2025 , the Government issued blocking orders for YouTube and Instagram handles of multiple Pakistani celebrities, YouTube channels that post Pakistani dramas and news, and even websites of Indian news organisations. During this period, X’s Global Government Affairs team (formerly Twitter) highlighted that the Government had instructed it to block over 8,000 accounts since the attack. It had mentioned that in most content takedown cases, the Centre had not specified which posts from an account had violated India’s local laws, and for a significant number of the accounts, the Government didn’t provide any evidence or justification to block the accounts. It is unclear whether the Centre provided the same for the blocking orders against Savala Vada. Why it matters: This lengthy list of content-blocking orders raises concerns about freedom of speech and expression online. Interestingly, during the legal challenge around the fact-check unit that the Government sought to introduce as part of the IT Rules, 2023, the Centre had argued that it would not fact-check satire or muzzle opinion. For context, this proposed fact-check unit was eventually struck down by the Bombay High Court . “If [petitioners] are afraid of their views being muzzled, they need not be. [Rule 3(1)(b) of the amended IT Rules ] does not deal with views, opinions, criticisms, humour, satire, or even abuses. Abuses also would not fall within fake or false. You can abuse the Prime Minister of the country, the Chief Minister of the state, anyone,” Tushar Mehta , who is currently the Additional Solicitor General Of India, said during a hearing of the case last year. However, based on the rampant rise of content takedowns, it seems that even without the fact-check unit, satire and commentary may be under threat in India. Legal provisions for blocking content in India: The Government has the power to block content under Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000. This section allows for content blocking orders in the interest of: The sovereignty and integrity of India Friendly relations with foreign states Public order Preventing incitement to the commission of any cognizable offence To issue these blocking orders, the Government has to comply with a range of procedural safeguards under the Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards for Blocking for Access of Information by Public) Rules , 2009. This includes a requirement to record the reasons for the blocking order in writing, a pre-decisional hearing where the platform (which hosted the content) and the user who posted it can participate, and a post-decisional review of the order. Besides Section 69A, the government also relies on Section 79(3)(b) of the IT Act to issue content blocking requests. Section 79 provides platforms with protection from liability for the content users post on their service. Part (3)(b) of Section 79 states that platforms can lose this immunity if they fail to remove unlawful content after being notified by the Government or its agencies. This section is currently under debate as part of an ongoing lawsuit between X and the Indian Government . It is unclear which section the Centre relied on to block Savala Vada’s Instagram page. Also read: Free speech debate: India orders X to block 8000+ accounts following Pahalgam attack Full List: Digital Censorship After Terrorist Attack in Kashmir India Blocks 16 Pakistani YouTube Channels After Pahalgam Terrorist Attack The post India Blocks Satirical Page ‘Savala Vada’ On Instagram, Raising Free Speech Concerns appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Lessons from Operation Sindoor’s global outreach
articles.summary: The intensive period of public diplomacy affirmed that India, when united, can project its voice with clarity and conviction on international platforms

articles.title: Dark Patterns and weak remedies: Why the CCPA Advisory falls short
articles.summary: The Central Consumer Protection Authority has issued an advisory directing all e-commerce platforms to conduct self-audits & eliminate “dark patterns.” Questions remain about the advisory’s enforceability and what remedies will ultimately address the growing use of these deceptive practices.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr India’s consumer watchdog, the Central Consumer Protection Authority, has issued an advisory dated June 5, 2025 (effective June 6, 2025) directing all e-commerce platforms to self-audit and eliminate “dark patterns”. However, without clear penalties or statutory backing, enforcement remains uncertain. Why should you care? Dark patterns affect everyone who uses digital platforms, whether you are shopping online, subscribing to a service, or ordering food. These deceptive design tactics trick you into making choices you did not intend to, such as signing up for unwanted subscriptions, missing hidden charges, or giving consent without realising it. They undermine your right to make informed decisions and can cost you time, money, and privacy. Background On 5 June 2025 the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) issued an advisory ordering e-commerce platforms to eliminate “dark patterns.” The CCPA, created by the Consumer Protection Act of 2019, policies unfair trade practices and misleading advertisements. Dark patterns are interface tricks—pre-ticked boxes, fake countdowns, hidden fees, hard-to-find cancellations—that push users into choices they would not freely make ("Only 1 left!"). They appear in food-delivery apps, news sites, digital-lending portals, and quick-commerce services, often violating the Consumer Protection Act (2019) and the E-Commerce Rules (2020). The Department of Consumer Affairs had already addressed the problem in November 2023 with its Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns. The Guidelines cover all online platforms—websites, apps, sellers, and advertisers—and brand 13 tactics (including false urgency, basket sneaking, confirm-shaming, and forced continuity) as unfair trade practices. Platforms must drop these designs and restore genuine user choice [read more about the Guidelines and our comments here ]. Regulatory Action in Practice: BookMyShow & IndiGo Airlines Recent enforcement actions by the CCPA illustrate how these Guidelines are already being tested on the ground. BookMyShow: The ‘Basket Sneaking’ Case BookMyShow was found automatically adding ₹1 per ticket toward its BookASmile charity initiative without consumer consent. This practice, enabled via pre-ticked checkboxes, violated Clause (2) of Annexure 1 of the 2023 Guidelines explicitly termed as "Basket Sneaking". CCPA’s Intervention: BookMyShow was issued a notice dated February 11, 2025, and subsequently modified its UI, now giving users a clear opt-in choice for donations. A small charge might seem minor, but when aggregated there is clear harm to thousands, if not lakhs of users over a month. IndiGo Airlines: From ‘Confirm Shaming’ to Transparent Choice The CCPA also issued a notice dated June 19, 2024, to IndiGo Airlines for two significant dark patterns: Confirm Shaming : Passengers opting out of add-ons were shown the message “No, I will take risk” . This kind of manipulative phrasing coerces users by implying irresponsibility or danger, a textbook case of confirm shaming. After CCPA’s notice , IndiGo changed the wording to a neutral alternative: “No, I will not add to the trip” . Opaque Seat Selection Process : The airline also obscured the ability to skip paid seat selection, nudging users toward preferential (paid) seating. Following CCPA’s direction, IndiGo updated its UI to include a disclaimer beside the “Skip” button: “You can skip preferred seat selection and complete your booking. IndiGo will auto-assign a seat prior to your travel.” What does the June Advisory say? In light of the increasing complaints and breach of the Guidelines, the CCPA has directed, vide the Advisory dated June 5, 2025, issued under Section 18(1) of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, all e-commerce platforms to conduct a thorough self-audit within three months of the advisory’s issuance. This audit must identify any such patterns and implement corrective measures to ensure consumer interactions are fair, ethical, and transparent. The advisory refers to the Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns, 2023, and is an extension of these guidelines, thereby serving as a tool for their interpretation. The Advisory also highlights Rule 4(9) of the E-Commerce Rules, 2020, which mandates that user consent must be obtained through clear and affirmative action. Furthermore, the CCPA encourages platforms to submit self-declarations confirming that they are not engaging in any dark pattern practices. Is the Advisory enforceable? Effective from June 6, 2025, and valid through December 31, 2026, the Advisory sets out a forward-looking framework for enhancing consumer protection in the digital economy. Although framed as an advisory, it functions as an extension of the existing Guidelines issued under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, and thus carries enforceable weight. While the Advisory may appear non-binding in form, it operates within the enforceable framework of the Guidelines, which are backed by the Consumer Protection Act under Sections 2(47), 20, and 21. Section 2(47) defines “unfair trade practices,” which includes misleading and manipulative tactics like dark patterns. Section 20 authorises the CCPA to recall goods, withdraw services, or stop such practices, while Section 21 enables it to penalise and ban misleading advertisements. Additionally, Section 18(2)(l) allows the CCPA to issue enforceable guidelines to prevent unfair trade practices Notably, in a recent judgment, National Restaurant Association of India v. Union of India , the Delhi High Court held that CCPA guidelines issued under Section 18(2)(l), such as those governing service charges, are mandatory and enforceable, reinforcing the seriousness with which such advisories must be treated. The Court stated: “ The CCPA is the authority fully empowered and has the jurisdiction to pass the guidelines under the CPA, 2019. In fact, issuing guidelines in consumer interest is an essential function of CCPA under Section 18(2)(l) of the CPA, 2019. The said guidelines would have to be mandatorily complied with as the scheme of the Act clearly provides for enforcement of guidelines; ” Joint Working Group On June 5, the Department of Consumer Affairs to improve enforcement against dark patterns, established a Joint Working Group (“Group”). Chaired by the Department’s Additional Secretary, the Group will run for 12 months, meet regularly, and submit periodic reports to the government. Its members include officials from key ministries, CCPA representatives, legal scholars, and consumer-rights advocates. The Group’s mandate is to spot violations, report them to the Department, propose consumer safeguards, and devise public-awareness strategies. While a positive development towards collaborative enforcement, yet the lack of voices from the digital-rights community, tech industry, and online platforms may limit the practicality of its recommendations. Worse, without a clear plan for implementing or enforcing those recommendations, the Group risks remaining merely consultative instead of driving real change. A Good Start, But Not Quite Enough The Department of Consumer Affairs’ Advisory is a welcome milestone in curbing dark patterns across India’s digital ecosystem. By telling platforms to self-audit, report, and remove deceptive design tactics, it takes an overdue step to protect consumers. Yet several gaps blunt its impact. First, the final 2023 Guidelines drop Clause 8, which would have made every breach of the Guidelines a breach of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. Without that linkage, Clause 4 still forbids dark patterns, but penalties are undefined. The CCPA can act under Sections 2(47), 20, and 21 of the Act, yet the missing statutory trigger makes enforcement uncertain and slow. Second, the Advisory offers no roadmap for smaller platforms, start-ups, or sector regulators. Large firms can afford thorough audits; smaller ones may not know where to begin. The Guidelines also exclude industry participation in monitoring or updating the standards, risking a gulf between regulation and evolving design practices. In short, the Advisory recognises the harm of dark patterns, but its long-term value depends on real enforcement, inclusive policymaking, and clear accountability. How to report Dark Patterns? You can report suspected dark patterns through the Department of Consumer Affairs’ Jagriti App. Enter the URL of any site or e-commerce platform you believe is using deceptive design tactics banned by the Guidelines. Your submission is filed as a formal complaint with the CCPA, which can then investigate and take action. Screenshot of the e-Jagriti mobile application taken on June 19, 2025 To improve enforcement, the CCPA is deploying the Jagriti Dashboard, a real-time monitoring tool that scans e-commerce URLs for dark patterns and flags suspicious sites. Paired with consumer reports from the Jagriti App, the Dashboard lets the CCPA detect, regulate, and act against deceptive online practices swiftly and precisely. Screenshot of the e-Jagriti dashboard taken on June 19, 2025 Important links Advisory by the Central Consumer Protection Authority [ Link ] Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 [ Link ] Consumer Protection Act, 2019 [ Link ] E-Commerce Rules, 2020 [ Link ]

articles.title: Striking Silence: How YouTube’s Broken Copyright System Threatens Free Speech
articles.summary: Misuse of YouTube’s copyright tools by ANI reveals how the platform's enforcement mechanisms are stifling free speech and critical journalism in India.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In May 2025, The Reporters’ Collective revealed that the news agency ANI was targeting YouTubers with copyright strikes. These incidents highlighted the inconsistencies in YouTube’s copyright enforcement and raised some valid concerns about the lack of transparency and adequate safeguards for creators. Why should you care? When copyright enforcement on platforms like YouTube lacks transparency and fails to reflect Indian legal principles it creates an uneven playing field. Content creators, especially those engaged in criticism, satire, education, or news, are left vulnerable to misuse of takedown tools and coercive demands. This undermines free expression, chills public discourse, and distorts the balance copyright law is meant to uphold. Ensuring that enforcement mechanisms align with domestic law is not just a legal issue, it is essential for protecting creativity, accountability, and democratic engagement online. Background The Reporter’s Collective on May 19, 2025 broke the story that News agency Asian News International (“ANI”) has issued copyright strikes against multiple journalists on YouTube for using clips from its copyrighted content. In several instances, ANI also demanded financial compensation in exchange for withdrawing the copyright strikes, prompting concerns that copyright enforcement was being used as a means of monetary coercion. This triggered a wave of responses from YouTubers, many of whom came forward to share similar experiences of facing pressure or demands in exchange for strike retractions. In one such video, content creator Mohak Mangal alleged that news agency ANI issued him two copyright strikes for using brief clips from their footage – one of which lasted only nine seconds. He shared screenshots and emails, purportedly from ANI, claiming they initially demanded ₹48 lakh in penalties and licensing fees to remove the strikes, and threatened additional strikes that could lead to his channel being shut down. He cited one specific instance on May 20, when he received a copyright strike for a video on the Kolkata rape case, in which he used just 11 seconds of ANI footage within a 16-minute video. As a platform with immense reach and influence, YouTube bears a responsibility to ensure that its copyright enforcement mechanisms are not only compliant with applicable law but also fair, transparent, and resistant to abuse. As you are aware, under YouTube's current copyright enforcement mechanism, creators may receive three copyright strikes in rapid succession resulting in permanent channel deletion. While we acknowledge that copyright enforcement is critical to protecting intellectual property, the current structure lacks transparency and safeguards. There is a troubling lack of clarity about how YouTube applies fair use principles in India, which we believe emerges from a lack of incorporation of local legal standards in enforcement practices. YouTube’s Copyright policy YouTube’s copyright enforcement system is largely automated, relying on tools like Content ID and user-submitted takedown notices. While this automation is arguably necessary at scale, it has created a system that often prioritizes claimants, typically large rights holders, at the expense of smaller creators. Three main tools which are part of the Copyright Management System of YouTube: the webform, the Copyright Match Tool, and Content ID. All of these tools use technology to prevent the reupload of matching content. Excerpt from YouTube’s Copyright Transparency Report for 2024 Under YouTube’s current policy, a channel that receives three copyright strikes within 90 days is terminated within seven days. These actions can be taken even when the underlying claims are contestable, without offering creators an adequate opportunity to respond before irreversible penalties are imposed. This approach bypasses the principle of audi alteram partem —a cornerstone of natural justice that requires both sides to be heard before a decision is made. The absence of a pre-decisional hearing undermines creators' right to a fair process and opens the door to potential misuse. While YouTube references fair use doctrines from jurisdictions like the EU, UK, Australia, and Canada, its policies remain vague on how Indian copyright law is applied on the platform in India. Under Indian law, the Copyright Act, 1957 provides clear exceptions under Section 52, including the “fair dealing” clause that protects content used for: Parody and satire Commentary and criticism News reporting Educational purposes However, YouTube's enforcement does not appear to reflect these domestic legal protections, leaving Indian creators vulnerable to takedowns and demonetization, often without explanation. However, what remains consistent in YouTube’s policy is its failure to explain the rationale behind such decisions. This lack of transparency forces creators to live with the consequences, often at the mercy of claimants whose motives may not always be in good faith. Following The Reporters’ Collective’s expose, many prominent YouTubers have gone public, describing ANI’s business of copyright strikes as ‘extortion’ despite Indian copyright rules allowing content makers to use a tiny bit of copyrighted visual under fair-use doctrine for making something entirely new. Here we would like to draw attention to the Reporters’ Collective in its May 27, 2025, report highlighting Ramit Verma’s case, the creator of the satirical YouTube channels Official PeeingHuman and Kroordarshan . He faced copyright violation notices from ANI targeting 14 of his videos. YouTube initially responded to ANI expressing concern that the takedown requests might not be valid, citing the "fair dealing" provisions under Indian copyright law that protect parody, commentary, and news reporting. Despite claiming it does not adjudicate copyright disputes, YouTube demanded further information from ANI to assess whether Ramit's videos qualified as fair dealing. After ANI submitted a detailed response, YouTube reviewed the material and ultimately rejected ANI’s claims for 12 of the videos, stating explicitly to both parties that the content was protected under fair dealing or similar exceptions, and would remain online. However, in the case of two videos, YouTube sided with ANI and blocked them. When Ramit contested this, asserting that these too were covered by fair dealing, YouTube did not provide a transparent rationale for the differing outcome. Such inconsistent enforcement demonstrates the lack of institutional checks and arbitrary case by case outcomes. Such lack of transparency contributes to a climate of fear and self-censorship, especially for creators engaging in political commentary, satire, or critique. Many creators feel they are at the mercy of claimants, some of whom may act in bad faith to silence dissent or competition. The company has defended its redressal mechanism by stating that the users can resolve copyright strikes in one of three ways: by submitting a valid counter-notice, obtaining a retraction from the original claimant, or allowing the strike to expire automatically after 90 days, provided no additional strikes occur during that period. Although these options exist, they are often burdensome, opaque, or ineffective, particularly when claimants refuse to engage or YouTube fails to act impartially. Excerpt from YouTube's page on Copyright and Rights Management The other legal framework to consider, which YouTube abides by, is the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (“DMCA”). The DMCA provides for both copyright claims and counter-claims. For instance, if someone files a copyright infringement claim against an individual for using their footage, the platform must notify the individual. If the claim is not contested, the platform is obligated to remove the content. However, if it is disputed then the platform must restore the content and is no longer liable—the copyright owner would then need to pursue legal action against the individual to resolve the issue. Notably, the DMCA does not require a three-strike policy; this is a rule YouTube has chosen to implement independently, without any legal obligation. YouTubers have also alleged that three strikes are being issued simultaneously in some cases, after allowing these alleged violations to accumulate, and thus there is a threat of getting banned from YouTube forever. There is also a claim that there is a penalty being charged on them for these alleged infringements, over and above the annual subscription fee. According to YouTube’s transparency report for 2024 : “Over 6% of videos requested for removal through the public webform in 2024 were the subject of abusive copyright removal requests, meaning these requests were assessed and rejected by our review team as a likely false assertion of copyright ownership.” The above mentioned examples highlight how most YouTubers are unaware of how copyright strikes and the Content ID system actually function, highlighting the platform’s failure to adequately educate users and raise awareness about these critical processes. Therefore, there is a need for clear legal standards, pre-decisional hearings, and transparent enforcement mechanisms on YouTube's Indian platform so that creators' rights are protected. The Fair Dealing principle in India India’s Copyright Act, 1957, includes an exception for “fair dealing” under Section 52. This provision allows for the use of copyrighted material without permission for specific purposes such as criticism, review, news reporting, and education. In Wiley Eastern Ltd. v. IIM [61 (1996) DLT 281], the Delhi High Court made an explicit link between fair dealing and the constitutional guarantee of free speech under Article 19(1)(a). The judgment held that the purpose of Section 52 was to preserve academic and public interest freedoms such as research, criticism, and reporting positioning copyright exceptions as part of the broader constitutional framework protecting human dignity and free expression. The list is purpose-driven, unlike the broader four-factor test of “fair use” under U.S. law. It ensures that copyright law does not unduly stifle creativity, free speech, or access to information, thereby maintaining a necessary balance between the rights of creators and the interests of society. The Indian courts over the years have developed several tests and principles to ascertain fair dealing under Section 52 of the Copyright Act, the most prominent of which include the following: The Three-fold Test In Blackwood and Sons Ltd. v. A.N. Parasuraman [1958 SCC OnLine Mad 62], the Madras High Court addressed the scope of fair dealing under Indian copyright law. The case involved the unauthorised reproduction of significant portions of copyrighted textbooks in guidebooks sold to students. The court rejected the defense of fair dealing, holding that the reproduction was not for private or personal use but was commercially motivated and substantially copied key parts of the original works. In its reasoning, the court developed a three-fold test to assess fair dealing: the purpose of the use—whether it is genuinely for private study, research, criticism, or review, or for commercial gain; the quantity and value of the material taken—whether a substantial or essential part of the original is reproduced; and the effect on the market—whether the copying competes with or undermines the market of the original work. Indian courts have consistently underscored that the determination of what constitutes fair dealing must be guided by the purpose and character of the use, the nature of the work, the amount and substantiality of the portion used, and the effect of the use on the potential market for the original work. These principles resonate with the tests laid down in foreign jurisdictions, particularly the United States, though Indian law is more prescriptive in the specific categories it recognises under fair dealing. De-minimis principle These principles are further refined by the de minimis doctrine, which enables courts to disregard trivial instances of copying that have no commercial or expressive significance. Although not codified, Indian courts have applied this doctrine as a threshold filter to exclude negligible copying even before engaging in the more elaborate fair dealing analysis. Though not explicitly codified in Indian law, the de minimis principle has found recognition in judicial reasoning, especially in cases where the copied portion is so incidental or fleeting that it neither competes with the original nor affects its commercial value. It complements fair dealing by offering an additional filter for excluding inconsequential uses from the scope of infringement. The Hon’ble Delhi High Court in India TV Independent News Service (P) Ltd . v. Yashraj Films (P) Ltd. [(2013) 53 PTC 586] held that brief use of a song in a consumer awareness ad and a singer's partial performance during a TV interview were both trivial and fell under 'fair use' through the doctrine of de minimis , which excludes insignificant uses from infringement claims. Originality Threshold Over the years the copyright law has evolved to apply to both original and derivative works. Copyright does not protect mere ideas or trivial expressions, rather it protects original works that reflect a minimum degree of creativity and the author’s intellectual input. As a result, if a copied element lacks sufficient originality, or if the defendant’s use is too insubstantial to impinge on the original’s creative expression, then copyright infringement may not arise at all. In this sense, the originality threshold interacts with the fair use principle to shape not just what is protected, but also what qualifies as infringement. For original works, the question of protection is straightforward, but derivative works such as compilations, or databases, pose trickier questions. In V. Govindan v. E.M. Gopalakrishna Kone [1954 SCC OnLine Mad 368], the Madras High Court held that the amount of originality required in case of a compilation is not very high and even a ‘small’ amount of originality is entitled to protection. To check if the work is original or not, the courts have established certain criteria over the years: The Sweat of Brow In line with the English judgment of Ladbroke v. Hill [(1964) 1 WLR 273], which established the ‘sweat of the brow’ doctrine as a test of originality based on skill, effort, and labor invested rather than creativity alone, Indian courts have similarly applied this principle. For instance, in Rai Toys Industries v. Munir Printing Press [1982 PTC 85], the Delhi High Court affirmed that even without high creativity, the diligent work was sufficient to grant the compilation original literary work protection under copyright law even though it may have been derived from common sources such as telephone directories and other public sources. Modicum of Creativity There was a clear shift in the Indian approach to originality with the Eastern Book Co . v. D.B. Modak [(2008) 1 SCC 1] judgment, where the Supreme Court departed from the traditional ‘sweat of the brow’ doctrine. It held that mere investment of capital and labour was not sufficient to claim copyright in derivative works. Instead, the Court emphasised that originality requires the application of skill and judgment. This marked a turning point in Indian copyright law, establishing a higher standard for originality. Our Representation to YouTube In the context of YouTube’s copyright practices, there is an urgent need for the platform to align its copyright enforcement mechanisms with Indian legal standards. The fair dealing exception under Section 52, the de minimis principle, and the originality threshold are vital safeguards that protect creators’ rights and freedom of expression. Yet, YouTube’s current system – driven by automated tools and opaque processes – often overlooks these principles, leaving creators vulnerable to misuse of copyright claims. It empowers major copyright holders while sidelining individual creators, often stifling fair use and critical speech. By automating copyright enforcement without transparency or due process, it functions as a private legal system driven by conflicting profit incentives. To ensure fairness, YouTube must incorporate clear, India-specific guidelines, provide pre-decisional hearings, and establish a more transparent and accountable dispute resolution process. Without these reforms, copyright enforcement will continue to act not as a shield for rights holders, but as a sword against legitimate speech. We have written to Mr. Rajesh Ranjan, Head of Government Affairs and Public Policy at Google, highlighting key aspects of Indian legislation and jurisprudence on fair dealing and the right to free expression. In our letter, we have urged Google to reform YouTube’s copyright enforcement system in India by: (1) clarifying the fair dealing tests and objective criteria used under Indian copyright law, and sharing these with creators in advance of any penalties; (2) updating the Copyright Help page and related terms to reflect India-specific fair dealing provisions, along with publishing a detailed explainer on YouTube’s Public Policy Blog; and (3) improving the redressal process through a transparent, timely counter-notice system that complies with Indian legal standards and includes safeguards prior to the termination of channels under the three-strike rule. Important documents Letter to Mr. Rajesh Ranjan, Head of Government Affairs and Public Policy at Google dated June 4, 2025 [ Link ]

articles.title: BBC Accuses Perplexity of Using Its Content Without Permission, Threatens Legal Action
articles.summary: BBC accuses Perplexity of copyright violations and demands content removal and compensation. The post BBC Accuses Perplexity of Using Its Content Without Permission, Threatens Legal Action appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The BBC has threatened to take legal action against AI news aggregator Perplexity for allegedly creating verbatim reproductions of BBC reports. In a report about the development, the BBC stated that it had written to Perplexity, demanding the company cease using BBC content, delete any existing content, and propose financial compensation for the content used so far. Based on its research, the BBC claims that popular chatbots (including Perplexity) inaccurately summarize news stories, which is highly damaging to its reputation and could undermine trust in its content. Some context: This is not the first time Perplexity has ended up in hot water over potential copyright violations. In June 2024 , Forbes accused the aggregator of plagiarising it and other news publications. Soon after, WIRED conducted an investigation that found Perplexity was still crawling websites even after they had blocked it. Notably, the BBC also flagged issues with Perplexity’s web crawler not respecting robots.txt files, stating that it had disallowed two of the crawlers from accessing its site. Amidst the pushback from publications over alleged copyright violations, in August 2024, the company signed a revenue-sharing deal with select news platforms, including TIME, Der Spiegel, Fortune, Entrepreneur, and The Texas Tribune. However, it is still engaged in a lawsuit against News Corp (which owns publications such as The Wall Street Journal and The New York Post). News Corp has demanded up to $150,000 per work of infringement, damages, and the profits Perplexity made from said infringement. The debate around AI and copyright in the UK: In December last year , the UK government conducted a consultation on AI and copyright. The consultation proposed three different approaches to tackle the growing concerns of copyright holders and AI developers. These were: Strengthening copyright protections by requiring AI companies to obtain a license for training their models on copyrighted work. Allowing developers to train on copyrighted works without the rights holder’s consent. Allowing developers to train models on copyrighted works unless the rights holder proactively opts out. This would require copyright holders to include machine-readable versions of the reservation of rights within their works to ensure that AI companies are aware of their preferences. The government appeared to favour the third option. As per a recently passed Data Use and Access Bill , the UK Secretary of State is required to conduct an economic assessment of the policy measures suggested in the December consultation. How is India looking at AI and copyright protections? OpenAI is currently engaged in a copyright infringement lawsuit filed by ANI in India. This lawsuit has brought to the fore many aspects of the copyright debate, including whether storing copyright-protected data amounts to infringement and whether using copyrighted data to generate responses qualifies as fair use . The decision in this case could shape how Indian courts interpret copyright law in the context of AI models. While the courts deliberate on the current law’s stance on AI data scraping, the Indian government has formed a multi-stakeholder committee to examine the intersection of AI and copyright. This committee is assessing whether the existing law is sufficient to address legal issues arising from AI’s use. Also read: Perplexity inks Revenue-Sharing Deal with Major News Publishers News Corp Sues Perplexity AI for Copyright Infringement, Seeks $150K Per Violation The New York Times Issues Cease and Desist notice to Perplexity for AI Scraping The post BBC Accuses Perplexity of Using Its Content Without Permission, Threatens Legal Action appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: We condemn terror – but blanket bans are not the answer
articles.summary: Read our statement on the advisory released by the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting on May 8, 2025, ordering intermediaries to remove all content originating in Pakistan.
articles.content: The Internet Freedom Foundation mourns the lives lost in the April 22, 2025, Pahalgam attack and affirms that national security is a paramount constitutional duty, yet insists that security measures themselves must remain within constitutional limits. The Ministry of Information and Broadcasting’s May 8, 2025, “advisory” ordering every OTT service, streaming platform and intermediary in India to remove all content “having its origins in Pakistan” exceeds those limits. Advisory on content originating from Pakistan In light of the Pahalgam terror attack, national security takes precedence. OTT platforms are hereby mandated to remove all content originating from Pakistan. #OperationSindoor #IndiaFightsPropaganda pic.twitter.com/fDBMWqLjBX — Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (@MIB_India) May 8, 2025
