articles.title: Ahmedabad Plane Crash : PIL Filed In Supreme Court For Grounding Of Air India's Boeing Fleet Till Safety Clearance
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: In the wake of the tragic Air India plane crash that took place in Ahmedabad on June 12, claiming 270 lives, a public interest litigation has been file before the Supreme Court seeking suspension of the airlines' Boeing fleet till appropriate safety and security audits are conducted. The PIL, filed by Advocate Ajay Bansal, alleges that the Aircraft Act, 1934, the Aircraft Rules, 1937, and civil aviation requirements are not being adhered to by commercial airlines. It seeks surprise safety audits across the entire Air India fleet and other commercial airlines operating in the country. The findings of such audits should be made public and penalties imposed for non-compliance with relevant rules, the petitioner says. He further seeks laying down of guidelines for safety and periodic checks of aircraft engines, airframes and cabin systems. "Air India's service and safety failures jeopardise passenger lives and comfort, contravene DGCA safety audits, and breach statutory duties under Section 5 & 7 of the Aircraft Act, 1934" claims the petition. In support of the contentions, it cites findings from a Directorate General of Civil Aviation inspection report, as per which Air India was found to have falsified internal safety audit records. Notably, the petitioner recounts in the petition his own unsatisfactory experience in the business class of an Air India flight he took on May 20 from Delhi to Chicago. He contends that the seats did not recline properly, the in-flight entertainment systems were not working, and the air conditioning system was ineffective. Despite a formal complaint, it is alleged that the airline offered a compensation of only Rs.10,000. Pointing to repeated technical lapses in Air India planes and social media posts complaining about the same, the petition adds that the June 12 crash, involving an 11-year-old Boeing 787-8 Dreamliner aircraft, was not an isolated tragedy, "but the consequence of chronic neglect, regulatory laxity, and an airline culture that has prioritised operational expedience over passenger safety". In related news, two doctors have written a letter to the Chief Justice of India seeking suo motu action by the Supreme Court with respect to the Ahmedabad plane crash. This letter seeks directions to the Central Government to disburse compensation to the victims at the earliest and a thorough investigation to ascertain the cause of the crash. Related - Air India Flight Crash At Ahmedabad : Law On Victim Compensation Explained

articles.title: Your Internet, Their Rules: Airtel's AI-driven internet filter poses censorship risks
articles.summary: Framed as fraud prevention, Airtel’s AI-based blocking system enables unchecked censorship, threatening user autonomy, privacy, and an open internet.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr Airtel’s new AI-based website blocking system designed to detect and block “fraudulent” websites goes beyond the licensing conditions under which ISPs operate in India. It raises serious concerns about net neutrality, privacy, and unchecked censorship. Why should you care? Because this affects your internet . Airtel is deciding, using AI, which websites you can and cannot visit without asking, informing, or allowing you to opt out. While the goal is to block scams, this may violate your digital rights and open the door to overreach, censorship, and invasive surveillance — all without public oversight. Background Amid the surge in digital fraud cases in India, on May 15, 2025, Airtel announced the launch of an AI-based security system designed to detect and block “fraudulent” websites. Screenshot of Bharti Airtel’s official press release dated May 15, 2025, retrieved from the company’s website on May 21, 2025 As per the press release this security service will cover all communications and over-the-top (“OTT”) platforms, emails, web browsers and SMS. It is claimed that the AI-powered security platform uses a multi-layered system to scan internet traffic in real time, using global threat databases and its own threat actor repository to block malicious websites across devices and platforms. It assigns a spam score to websites using internal signals and threat intelligence from sources such as global repositories and banks; high scores lead to the site being blocked before it loads. When a user attempts to access a flagged malicious site, the page is blocked and replaced with a notice explaining the reason. According to Airtel's MD Gopal Vittal, the tool has shown high accuracy in six months of trials and aims to make Airtel's networks fully secure from scams and spam. Screenshot of a pictorial representation from Bharti Airtel’s official press release dated May 15, 2025, retrieved from the company’s website on May 21, 2025 The new AI-powered blocking system, while aimed at security, could similarly undermine net neutrality principles by giving Airtel significant control over what content users can access potentially without transparent oversight or user consent. Moreover, India’s ISP market is highly oligopolistic, dominated by a few major players including Airtel, Jio, and Vodafone Idea, which amplifies the risks of such centralised control. When only a handful of companies manage the majority of internet traffic, policies like Airtel’s AI blocking platform could lead to inconsistent, arbitrary, or excessive content filtering, further limiting consumer choice and the free flow of information online. What does the law say? Development of Net Neutrality regulation in India The debate around net neutrality in India began gaining public attention in 2014, through one of the country’s most significant digital rights movements – SavetheInternet.in – which brought the issue into mainstream public discourse. In response, the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (“TRAI”) issued a consultation paper in 2015 on differential pricing for data services. Subsequently, TRAI released the “Prohibition of Discriminatory Tariffs for Data Services Regulations” in February 2016 . This regulation banned service providers from offering or charging different rates for data based on content, effectively disallowing zero-rating plans and paid prioritisation models. This was a landmark move, placing India among the early adopters of formal net neutrality protections. Following this, TRAI initiated a broader consultation on net neutrality in 2017, aimed at establishing a more comprehensive regulatory framework. Based on stakeholder feedback, TRAI released its final recommendations on November 28, 2017, advocating for strong net neutrality protections, including a ban on blocking, throttling, and paid prioritisation of content through amendments in the licensing terms for ISPs. These recommendations were formally accepted by the Department of Telecommunications (“DoT”) on July 31, 2018, as the “Regulatory Framework on Net Neutrality”, making it legally binding . The Regulatory Framework amended Unified Licenses for ISPs to incorporate net neutrality principles. However, DoT made some modifications, reserving for itself the role of defining Traffic Management Practices (“TMP”s), setting up a Multi-Stakeholder Body (“MSB”) for advisory purposes, and retaining monitoring and enforcement responsibilities. To address these additional aspects, DoT sought further recommendations from TRAI on July 31, 2018. TRAI responded by releasing a consultation paper in January 2020 , soliciting public input on the structure and functions of the MSB and the cataloging of reasonable TMPs. After extensive stakeholder discussions, TRAI submitted its final recommendations in January 2022. These included detailed guidelines on acceptable TMPs and proposed the formation of a multi-stakeholder advisory body to support the DoT in overseeing net neutrality compliance. Legality of Airtel’s actions (or lack thereof) The Unified License for Internet Service Providers is a license issued by the DoT that consolidates multiple telecom services, enabling providers to offer internet, telephony, and mobile services under a single framework. Clause 2.1 of this license guarantees subscribers “ unrestricted access to all content available on the Internet, except for content restricted by the Licensor or designated authority under law .” Building on this, the Regulatory Framework on Net Neutrality – based on recommendations from the TRAI – led to amendments in various internet service license agreements. These amendments were formalised by the DoT on September 26, 2018, as the Amendments to the Unified Licenses as per the Regulatory Framework , strengthening the licensing terms with Clause 2.3, which imposes a binding obligation on internet providers to treat all internet traffic non-discriminatorily, thereby embedding the core principles of net neutrality into the regulatory framework. It states: “ Principle of non-discriminatory treatment, definition of specialised services and reasonable traffic management and other exceptions: (i) A Licensee providing Internet Access Service shall not engage in any discriminatory treatment of content, including based on the sender or receiver. the protocols being used or the user equipment. (ii) The Licensee is prohibited from entering into any arrangement, agreement or contact, by whatever name called, with any person, natural or legal, that has the effect of discriminatory treatment of content. ” “Discriminatory treatment" has also been defined in the amendment [Clause 2.3 (iv) (b)] to include practices like blocking, degrading, slowing down or granting preferential speeds/treatment to any content. By embedding these principles into the licensing framework, the DoT ensured that net neutrality became an enforceable obligation, strengthening consumer protections and promoting a free and open internet in India. Additionally, the amendment Clause 2.3 (iii) (b) is an exception clause. Discriminatory treatment of internet content is generally prohibited, but it is allowed in limited and clearly defined cases under this exception clause. Such treatment is permissible when the Licensee adopts measures that are proportionate, temporary, and transparent, such as implementing reasonable traffic management, providing emergency services, or complying with legal orders from courts or government authorities. These exceptions ensure that while net neutrality is upheld, necessary flexibility is available for public safety, legal compliance, and efficient network management. Flowing from the above licensing conditions, the legal basis for blocking caused through the AI fraud detection system is not clear in law, as it does not emerge from any directions under Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000 or under a court order. Such a generalised blocking service at the level of the ISP constitutes an independent access restriction, and is beyond the actions permitted. Airtel’s plan to use AI filters may curb fraud and cyber-crime, but without strong legal safeguards and public oversight it invites abuse. An opaque system can favour some sites and block others, leading to hidden censorship, anti-competitive conduct, and suppression of lawful speech. These risks grow when no one knows the rules that guide the algorithm, there is no independent review, and users have no clear way to seek redress. In February 2025, TRAI amended its Telecom Commercial Communication Customer Preference Regulation (“TCCCPR”) to require AI-based detection of Unsolicited Commercial Communication (“UCC”) over telecom networks, allowing action against unregistered senders and repeated offenders — but only based on verified complaints and set thresholds. While Airtel has introduced AI tools to block malicious links and detect spam, including on apps like WhatsApp and Telegram, website blocking falls outside the scope of TCCCPR. Our concerns Airtel’s announcement of an AI-based security system raises deep concerns about user rights and access to the internet. Conflict with the Net Neutrality principle: At the heart of the controversy is the conflict with net neutrality, a principle that mandates equal treatment for all internet traffic, without discrimination or interference by service providers. Under Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, only the Central or State Government has the authority to order content to be blocked, and even that power must be exercised following strict legal procedures. ISPs do not possess unilateral authority to restrict access to online content. Any deviation from equal treatment of data – such as discriminatory blocking or throttling – is permissible solely under narrowly defined exceptions, such as reasonable traffic management or other legitimate grounds outlined in the Regulatory Framework [para 3.2.1]. Moreover, the Unified License explicitly mandates unrestricted access to internet content, as discussed above. With the 2018 amendment vide DoT’s Regulatory Framework, the obligation to uphold net neutrality is a formal licensing condition. This means any attempt by an ISP to block or discriminate against content, not backed by legal directive, is not just improper, but a direct breach of its license agreement. Lack of redressal and transparency : Compounding this problem is the lack of user autonomy. Subscribers are not offered the option to disable the service or override the blocking mechanism. The erosion of user autonomy is further compounded by the absence of a robust and transparent grievance redressal mechanism. While Airtel has acknowledged that users can report incorrect blocks, it has failed to disclose any details about the process. There is no information on how complaints are evaluated, the timeframes for resolution, or the criteria for reinstating access. In effect, users are left without procedural safeguards or clarity on outcomes. This lack of due process is especially problematic given the opaque, algorithm-driven nature of the blocking system, which is susceptible to false positives particularly in contexts involving nuanced political, cultural, or linguistic content. Without public access to the blocklist or a formal appeal system, users have no way of knowing why access was denied or whether the decision can be contested. This creates a black-box environment where private entities, without oversight, can restrict fundamental digital rights. Threat to privacy: The AI tool scans all forms of digital communication – ranging from emails and SMS to OTT platforms and web traffic – raising legitimate concerns about mass surveillance. Users are given no insight into what data is being analysed, how long it is stored, or whether it is shared with third parties. The absence of public oversight or an independent regulatory mechanism amplifies these worries, as users have no way to verify the tool's behavior or challenge wrongful censorship. In response to MediaNama’s query, Airtel stated that the tool does not log or store any user-level data. Centralisation of power According to the latest data from the T RAI as of December 31, 2024, the Indian market continues to be highly concentrated, with around 95% of subscribers served by just four providers. Reliance Jio Infocomm dominated with a 50.43% share, followed by Bharti Airtel at 30.62% and Vodafone Idea at 13.37%. Bharat Sanchar Nigam Limited held just 3.74% of the market, while the remaining fraction is split among smaller regional players. This overwhelming concentration of market power among a few telecom giants underlines the lack of real competition in India’s broadband landscape. In such an oligopolistic environment, users face limited options. If any of these dominant players adopt restrictive or flawed content filtering systems, such as AI-based blocking tools, the consequences are far-reaching. With so much control consolidated in so few hands there is a heightened risk of overreach. This centralisation of control not only threatens digital freedom but also undermines the principles of a free and open internet. This development sets a troubling precedent. Once the infrastructure for AI-based content blocking is established, its use can easily extend beyond fraud prevention to include the suppression of speech and dissent. In the absence of clear legal safeguards, such systems risk becoming instruments of surveillance and control rather than tools for public safety. These concerns are not merely hypothetical. A growing number of instances show licensed internet service providers in India blocking specific types of content with little transparency or accountability. A comprehensive study by the Centre for Internet and Society, published in January 2020, highlighted this trend by exposing major inconsistencies across ISP-maintained website blocklists. The findings suggest that ISPs are either failing to comply with official blocking orders or are arbitrarily restricting access without any legal authorisation. Our representation to Airtel Airtel’s AI-based content filtering system, while presented as a safeguard against digital fraud, raises serious questions about transparency, user autonomy, privacy, and the future of net neutrality in India. In a market dominated by a few powerful ISPs, such unchecked technological interventions risk creating a deeply imbalanced digital ecosystem where corporate decisions determine what users can access online. This move threatens to erode fundamental digital rights and set a precedent for broader forms of censorship and surveillance. We have written a letter to the Vice Chairman and Managing Director of Airtel, Mr. Gopal Vittal, to urge the development of a more transparent and participatory framework. Users should be informed, empowered to make their own choices, and given the option to opt in or out of such filtering mechanisms. This approach would not only better align with constitutional values but also safeguard user rights through accountability, legal clarity, and respect for individual autonomy. We have also filed RTIs with DoT and TRAI requesting information on any proposals, meetings, or communications between them and Airtel regarding their AI-based security system, including its legality, impact on privacy and net neutrality, and any related public consultations or internal documents. Ensuring transparency and accountability in such initiatives is crucial to preserving a free and open internet for all Indians. We remain committed to monitoring these developments closely and advocating for the protection of digital rights. Important documents Letter to Bharti Airtel dated May 23, 2025 [ Link ] Copy of RTI with the Department of Telecommunications dated May 23, 2025 [ Link ] Copy of RTI with the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India dated May 23, 2025 [ Link ] IFF’s previous work on Net Neutrality [ Link ]

articles.title: When KYC Becomes a Barrier: Supreme Court’s Stand for Digital Inclusion
articles.summary: In its April 30, 2025 judgment, the Supreme Court recognised the exclusion caused by E-KYC systems and held that digital access is a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In a judgement dated April 30, 2025 delivered in the case of Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors. , W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors ., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 the Supreme Court of India observed that the right to digital access is an intrinsic component of the right to life and liberty. This ruling and its accompanying observations serve as a strong foundation for future petitions advocating for greater digital inclusion. Why should you care? Digital technology has undoubtedly made many aspects of life easier but when systems rely solely on it, without offering alternatives, they can end up excluding the very people they are meant to serve. For many citizens, especially persons with disabilities and those from marginalised communities, the push for digital KYC has become a barrier rather than a bridge. But exclusion is not limited to the vulnerable and marginalized alone. Even educated and otherwise eligible individuals can be denied opportunities due to technical glitches. People are being denied basic services because of things as simple as a fingerprint mismatch, a technical error, or not having access to a smartphone or stable internet. This is not just a technical issue—it is a human one. A system that does not account for the needs of all kinds of people, including persons with disabilities, is not an inclusive system. Our Constitution promises equality to every citizen under Article 14. When policies leave out entire sections of society, especially the most vulnerable, it becomes essential to call that out and seek accountability. The Supreme Court’s recent judgment is a powerful reminder that digital access is not just a policy goal—it is part of our fundamental rights, and it must be protected as such. Background On 30th April, the Supreme Court in the case of Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 issued 20 directions aimed at ensuring that e-KYC and other digital public services are made “universally accessible.” In doing so, the Court underscored that digital inclusion is not merely a matter of convenience, but a constitutional imperative and declared digital access to be an intrinsic component to Right to Life and Liberty. This decision stemmed from two petitions filed by acid attack survivors, led by Pragya Prasun, and Amar Jain, a visually impaired lawyer. The petitions highlighted the challenges they face in accessing digital services, especially those that rely on facial recognition or eye movement for verification. The petitioners described how they are routinely excluded from essential services, as digital KYC processes frequently require actions such as facial movements or blinking—tasks that are not possible for many individuals with facial disfigurements or visual impairments. Financial institutions are required by law to conduct KYC (Know Your Customer) procedures to verify the identity and other details of their clients to crack down on fraudulent transactions. Aadhaar-based e-KYC enables the identity and address of a customer to be verified electronically via Aadhaar authentication, making the entire process paperless. The customer provides their explicit consent (by providing biometrics or OTP) for the e-KYC service to instantly generate an electronic, non-repudiable proof of identity and proof of address. This case is just one example where the process of authentication of identity verification has been digitised and the people have been excluded from the process. This reality also extends to various other marginalized populations who are getting excluded on a daily basis due to biometric mismatches and other Aadhaar based identity systems, which have been made mandatory. In this context, the judgment of the Supreme Court underscores the importance of digital access in today’s world and provides a solid basis for future petitions seeking to advance digital inclusion. Digital Identity Exclusion: The Persistent Problem The problem of digital identity exclusion has two key dimensions: one stems from authentication failures, while the other arises from the risk of being entirely removed from official records. In recent years, Aadhaar-based identity systems have been increasingly implemented by both Union and State government agencies across India to authenticate individuals for access to a wide range of public services. These systems are now widely used in public offices, hospitals, and schools. However, their deployment often lacks sufficient oversight and safeguards, posing significant risks to citizens’ sensitive data and potentially excluding those who lack access to or familiarity with digital technology. The debate regarding exclusion from access to services is not unique to e-KYC and has been raised several times, especially with regard to Aadhaar-based and biometric-linked identity systems. The aim of these systems is to verify the identity and address of an individual electronically, making the entire process paperless. The process commences with the customer providing their explicit consent (by providing biometrics or OTP) for the e-KYC service to instantly generate an electronic, non-repudiable proof of identity and proof of address. The government asserts that the digitalisation aims to enhance transparency, efficiency and saves time while safeguarding the rights of citizens. However, this has not been the reality. In the Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG) report on the functioning of UIDAI, 2021, it was acknowledged that the need for biometric update could arise on account of authentication failures due to incorrect biometric capture or poor biometric quality captured at the time of enrollment. Excerpt from the CAG report highlighting the problem of authentication failures Excerpt from the CAG report with recommendations Major instances of exclusion caused by technology have primarily resulted from fundamental issues in the authentication process. Recently, thousands of teenage ration card holders in Tamil Nadu were at risk of losing access to subsidised food grains under the National Food Security Act (NFSA) as outdated Aadhaar biometrics recorded years ago have stalled their e-KYC verification. Most of the affected teenagers are school students aged 13 to 17 from economically disadvantaged families. Their Aadhaar biometrics were recorded five to six years ago when they were under the age of 10, and the system now fails to recognise their fingerprints, official sources said. Similarly, beneficiaries above the age of 70 are facing verification issues due to faded fingerprints. Additionally, the e-KYC process has contributed to the exclusion of ration cardholders, especially among migrants. These concerns are not new, but they take on renewed urgency in light of the recent mandate requiring e-KYC updates. Individuals who fail to update their information risk being excluded from benefits under the Public Distribution System (PDS). As a result, many states are struggling to manage the e-KYC update process, even as millions have already been excluded from the food security net. This exclusion stems from the government's continued reliance on outdated data, largely due to delays in conducting the national census as well. In recent years, numerous reports have also highlighted cases of workers being wrongly marked as deceased in the Management Information System (MIS) of the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005 (NREGA). In reality, these individuals are very much alive but remain unaware that they have been removed from the system, a situation affecting many workers who were previously listed on the MGNREGS rolls without their knowledge. On January 01, 2024, the Union Rural Development Ministry made the use of Aadhaar-Based Payment System (“ABPS”) mandatory for the payment of wages to rural workers under the NREGA, and announced that non-compliance will be met with penalties. Further, the NREGA Mobile Monitoring System (“NMMS”) App which was made mandatory for workers to mark their attendance last year, continues to be inaccessible and inaccurate, costing them their daily wage. It has been pointed out repeatedly that as ABPS was made mandatory, NREGA account deletions shot up. In the 2024 Winter Session of Parliament, Congress MP K.C. Venugopal asked a question stating that 10.43 crore job card deletions had taken place under the NREGA scheme in the last four years across the country and sought to know whether it was because of the introduction of the Aadhaar-based payment system that became mandatory on January 1, 2024. According to data provided by LibTech India, a consortium of academics and activists, between April and September 2024, 84.8 lakh workers’ names were deleted from the scheme. Last year, LibTech data showed that 8 crore people were removed from the MGNREGA registry during the financial years 2022-23 and 2023-24. The true tragedy of these deletions lies not only in the loss of livelihood for millions of rural workers but also in the systemic failure to uphold the core principles of NREGA, a program intended to serve as a safety net for the most vulnerable members of society. In response to these allegations, the Union Minster replied by stating that the union government has no role in the deletion of job cards under the NREGA scheme and its responsibility lies with the state governments. Although state governments execute the deletions at the ground level, the Union government shares equal responsibility for the crisis due to its policy decisions, outdated guidelines, and failure to address underlying systemic problems. The Internet Freedom Foundation (IFF) , along with several other organisations and campaigns—most notably Rethink Aadhaar—has consistently raised concerns and written to various government departments about the risks of exclusion and privacy violations arising from the mandatory Aadhaar linkage for access to State welfare schemes. At IFF’s annual event, Privacy Supreme 2024 , held on August 22, 2024, Nikhil Dey, Founding Member of the Mazdoor Kisan Shakti Sangathan, shed light on the ground realities of the Aadhaar project. Through firsthand accounts, he highlighted how individuals have been excluded from pension rolls and other social security schemes, underscoring the harmful consequences of so-called “voluntary mandatory” schemes, particularly for rural communities. Therefore, implementing technological interventions in social security and welfare delivery—without first addressing the deep digital divide and low levels of internet literacy in the country—is a policy decision that inevitably leads to exclusion and infringes on the fundamental rights of countless individuals. Digital Divide The judgment also draws attention to the deep-rooted digital divide in India. The Court observed that individuals in rural and remote areas face poor internet connectivity, limited digital literacy, and a lack of regional language content—barriers that prevent effective access to e-governance and welfare services. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 The India Inequality Report 2022 highlights that 70% of the population either lacks or has inadequate access to digital services. Flagship initiatives like BharatNet, intended to expand rural connectivity, have fallen short of their targets. Among the poorest 20% of households, a mere 2.7% have access to a computer, and only 8.9% have internet connectivity. The report further reveals that only 38% of Indian households are digitally literate, with internet usage in rural areas standing at just 31%, compared to 67% in urban areas. Complementing this, data from the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India shows stagnation in wireless broadband subscriber growth. The trend highlights that a significant portion of the population still lacks the digital infrastructure needed for processes like e-KYC. Graph showing the number of subscribers every 4 months from 2020 to January 2025 The graph depicting wireless broadband subscribers in India every quarter from January 2020 to January 2025 shows a steady rise from approximately 650 million in January 2020 to just under 900 million by January 2025. A sharp increase is visible in the first half of 2020, with subscriber numbers jumping by nearly 50 million between January and July 2020—likely reflecting the urgent shift to digital infrastructure during the early pandemic. However, post-2021, growth becomes sluggish and uneven. Notably, between mid-2023 and mid-2024, subscriber numbers plateaued around 880 to 900 million, showing no substantial growth for nearly a year. This stagnation is not merely a statistical anomaly—it signals a deeper systemic issue: the calcification of digital divide. As access to wireless internet becomes critical for accessing education, welfare schemes, and employment, the lack of continued growth in wireless subscription suggests that large sections of the population are unable to cross the digital threshold. Key Takeaways from the Judgment The judgment authored by Justice Mahadevan clearly holds that the right to digital access is an essential part of the right to life and liberty under Article 21 of the Constitution. In today’s digital age, access to services like education, healthcare, banking, and welfare depends heavily on digital platforms. Yet, persons with disabilities, rural communities, senior citizens, women, and linguistic minorities continue to face serious exclusion due to inaccessible design, lack of digital literacy, poor connectivity, and limited regional language support. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 The Court specifically addressed the barriers in digital KYC systems, noting that visual tasks like facial recognition, CAPTCHA, or aligning one’s face are impossible for persons with visual impairments or facial disfigurements. As a result, they are often denied basic services. The Court affirmed that such exclusion violates both constitutional rights and disability laws like the RPwD Act, 2016 and the UNCRPD, and called for digital platforms—government and private—to comply with accessibility standards like the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG). Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 Importantly, the Court recognized the petitioners’ right to demand reasonable accommodations and emphasized that digital KYC guidelines must be revised with accessibility at their core. In doing so, it issued 20 specific directions to ensure digital KYC is accessible to persons with disabilities, especially those with visual impairments or facial/eye disfigurements due to acid attacks. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & W P (C ) No. 49 of 2025 This judgment is more than a legal win—it is a powerful affirmation that digital systems must serve everyone, not just the tech-savvy or able-bodied. As the India Inequality Report 2022 shows, digital gaps are also gendered—only 31% of women own phones compared to 61% of men. In a country where one failed fingerprint scan can cost someone their ration or wage, the Court’s recognition of digital access as a fundamental right is a step toward real, inclusive justice—but the true challenge lies in implementation. Even before this landmark judgment, various Courts had recognized the problems caused by biometric authentication failures and digital exclusion. But this is the first time the Supreme Court has directly addressed the issue from the perspective of persons with disabilities. For instance, the Madhya Pradesh High Court, in a judgment dated March 25, 2025, held that a person’s identity cannot be determined solely by a machine and that biometric failure cannot be grounds to deny legal rights. Courts across the country are now speaking in one voice: alternatives to biometric authentication must be provided. Excerpt from the judgment in W P No. 8990 of 2020 and WP No. 6148 of 2020 Such alternative systems are not just legal safeguards—they are lifelines for people excluded due to fingerprint mismatches, poor internet access, disabilities, or lack of digital literacy. The right to access public services should not depend on one’s ability to operate a smartphone or align their face within a frame—it should depend only on their identity as a citizen. Even in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India , (2019) 1 SCC 1, where the Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of the Aadhaar Act, it clearly recognized that exclusion from welfare benefits due to authentication failure is unacceptable. The Court noted that while the Aadhaar system aims to reduce leakages and ensure targeted delivery, it cannot come at the cost of denying benefits to those who genuinely qualify. It stressed that individuals who are unable to authenticate due to fingerprint mismatch, iris scan failure, or disability must be given alternate means to establish their identity. The judgment explicitly called for "suitable provisions" to prevent exclusion caused by technological limitations. Government needs to do more As the government intensifies the efforts in the digitisation of public services, it must also invest in strengthening offline support systems to ensure no one is excluded in the process. Common Service Centres (CSCs), set up under the Digital India initiative, are vital in bridging this gap. Located in rural and remote areas, CSCs enable citizens—especially those lacking internet access or digital literacy—to access essential services like ID registration, utility payments, and official certificates without having to travel to government offices. To truly make digital systems inclusive, CSCs must be equipped to provide alternative enrollment options and conduct outreach programs that build digital awareness, ensuring that the transition to a digital-first model leaves no one behind. This point becomes even more critical in the backdrop of the government's flagship scheme, the Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (“PMGDISHA”). Launched in 2017, PMGDISHA aimed to provide basic digital literacy to 60 million citizens across rural India within two years. Between 2019 and 2022, the Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (PMGDISHA) received consistent funding, with allocations of ₹400 crore in 2019–20, ₹300 crore in 2020–21, and ₹250 crore in both 2021–22 and 2022–23. However, from 2023–24 onwards, no funds have been allocated to the scheme, despite having not fully achieved its stated objectives. The discontinuation of such a vital programme, intended to empower the country's most digitally underserved communities, is deeply concerning. PMGDISHA played a key role in enabling rural citizens to access essential government, healthcare, and financial services online. Its absence leaves a significant gap in India’s push toward inclusive digital transformation. Another pressing issue the government must address is the lack of effective grievance redressal mechanisms within current systems. The Unique Identification Authority of India (“UIDAI”), set up a grievance redressal mechanism centrally to receive grievances through different channels which are highlighted below. Excerpt from the CAG report highlighting the Grievance Redressal Mechanism The CAG report highlighted a critical flaw in the existing grievance redressal mechanism: the inordinate delay in resolving complaints. One key issue is that grievances raised at the Regional Office level often fail to reach the attention of UIDAI headquarters, significantly undermining the system’s effectiveness. Additionally, the current grievance redressal process appears to be designed with the assumption that users have access to mobile phones—an assumption that excludes large sections of the population, particularly in rural and marginalized communities. This disconnect forces many citizens to run from pillar to post in search of resolution for issues that directly impact their access to essential services and rightful entitlements. What is urgently needed is a grievance redressal system underpinned by procedural safeguards, similar to those established in the Right to Information Act, 2005. Under RTI, there are clear timelines—such as a 30-day limit for the Public Information Officer to respond—followed by an appeals process that escalates to higher authorities, including the Central Information Commission. At every level, officials must provide a written, reasoned decision under their name, thereby ensuring transparency and accountability. In contrast, the current Aadhaar-linked grievance system lacks both responsiveness and accessibility. This not only erodes trust in public institutions but disproportionately harms the most vulnerable, who may not possess the digital access or bureaucratic literacy needed to navigate such systems. A rights-based, transparent, and inclusive grievance mechanism is not merely a technical fix—it is essential to safeguarding dignity, equity, and trust in public service delivery. Therefore, it is constitutionally imperative for the government to design systems that are not exclusively reliant on biometric or digital authentication. In light of the recent precedents, re-affirming digital access as part of the Right to Life and Liberty should compel policymakers to integrate inclusive alternatives into all digital public service systems—because a truly just and efficient welfare system does not punish citizens for the limitations of the very tools designed to help them. Important Documents: Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 ( Link ) Vinod Kumar Meena vs. Life Insurance Corporation of India, W.P. No. 8990 of 2020 ( Link )

articles.title: Prosus 2025 Report: Swiggy Reduces Loss By 30%, PayU Records 12% Revenue Growth
articles.summary: Global investor group Prosus in its 2025 report reported that Swiggy reduced its adjusted EBITDA loss by 30% and PayU saw a 12% revenue rise. The post Prosus 2025 Report: Swiggy Reduces Loss By 30%, PayU Records 12% Revenue Growth appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: Global investor group Prosus released its Annual Report 2025, detailing the performance of its investments in the e-commerce space. Prosus’ key companies in its India portfolio include Swiggy, Meesho, PayU, Urban Company, and Rapido, which are collectively valued at Rs. 1,81,912 crore ($21 billion), with Prosus holding shares worth Rs. 69,299 crore ($8 billion). How did Prosus’ major investments in India fare? Swiggy As per Prosus’ report, it sold around 109.1 million shares during Swiggy’s Rs 11,327 crore Initial Public Offering (IPO). Accordingly, it reduced its stake in Swiggy to less than 25%. Regarding its financial performance, the food delivery platform’s Gross Order Value (GOV) grew by 29% year-over-year (YoY). It reduced its Adjusted EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortisation) loss to Rs 1,568.05 crore ($182 million) from Rs 2,248.68 crore ($261 million) in the previous year. This growth was attributed to sustained momentum in food delivery, and the expansion of Swiggy’s quick commerce vertical, Instamart. Differing from its original approach of a unified interface, Swiggy has now launched Instamart as a standalone app as well. Notably, the growth was accompanied by challenges to profitability due to the expansion of the network and heightened competition. Overall, Swiggy aims to break even in the quick commerce segment within the next three to five quarters. payu Prosus also shed light on payment aggregator PayU, which finds its largest market in India. In FY25, India Payments recorded a revenue growth of 12% to Rs 4,290.6 crore or $498 million, driven by deeper penetration in existing merchants and growing value-added services like affordability. The growth continued despite competitive pressure and a higher UPI mix reducing take rates. Meanwhile, the total payment volume (TPV) rose by 14% owing to strong growth in financial services, government segments, airlines and food delivery. Consequently, India Payments reached breakeven during the second half of FY25. The company noted cost optimisation, scale leverage, and improvement in the aEBIT (adjusted earnings before interest and taxes) margin as the reasons behind this metric. Moving to its credit business, PayU Finance (PayU’s NBFC arm) offers unsecured personal loans to consumers and small and medium businesses (SMBs). Within this segment, new loan issuances stood at Rs. 9,528 crore ($1.1 billion), and SMB lending contributed 23% of this total. Besides this, revenue growth was 60% due to the growth in assets under management and diversification into merchant lending. Meanwhile, the aEBIT margin stood at -19% owing to higher financial leverage and higher-than-expected losses from the consumer loan book. In response, the company claimed to improve its underwriting practices from 2024 onwards. In a different vein, PayU India acquired a 70% stake in Mindgate Solutions for Rs 586 crore to improve its operational efficiencies. Growth continued despite India’s dynamic regulatory and competitive landscape, Prosus added. For context, PayU India added 13,000 new merchants after the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) lifted the 15-month embargo on onboarding new merchants in April 2024. Later, the company also received RBI authorisation to operate as a payment aggregator. Consequently, Prosus further delayed PayU’s plans for a public listing, instead focusing on business improvement over the next year, Reuters reported . Previously, in October 2024, PayU had deferred its IPO plans to early 2025 amid regulatory setbacks. Eyeing other public listings Prosus is also aiming for other public listings in India in FY26, as conveyed by Prosus Chief Executive Officer (CEO) Fabricio Bloisi to CNBC. Meesho: E-commerce platform Meesho recently concluded its reverse flip – which entails an overseas start-up shifting its domicile to India – and aims to file its draft red herring prospectus (DRHP) in the next two to three weeks, as reported by The Economic Times. Furthermore, the company plans to raise around Rs 8,662 crore via IPO, as per reports . Currently, Prosus holds a 13.2% stake in Meesho. Urban Company: At-home services marketplace Urban Company filed for a Rs 1,900 crore IPO. This offer included fresh shares worth Rs 429 crore and equity shares worth Rs 1,471 crore, and was a significant decrease from its original IPO of Rs 3,000 crore. Also Read: Swiggy’s Q4FY25 Losses Nearly Double to Rs 1,081 Cr Despite User Growth PayU Delays IPO Plans To 2025 Due To Regulatory Setbacks What Prosus Annual Report Tells us About the Financial Performance of Swiggy, BYJU’S and Meesho The post Prosus 2025 Report: Swiggy Reduces Loss By 30%, PayU Records 12% Revenue Growth appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Amid Legal Gray Area of Online Pharmacies, Amazon Expands into India’s At-Home Diagnostics
articles.summary: Amazon offers at-home lab tests in 6 Indian cities, expanding its health services amid regulatory uncertainty. The post Amid Legal Gray Area of Online Pharmacies, Amazon Expands into India’s At-Home Diagnostics appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The e-commerce giant Amazon has entered the at-home diagnostics market, according to a company press release . The company is expanding its existing services for online medicine delivery and teleconsultation, positioning itself as a multi-service healthcare platform within its e-commerce app. These services now operate in six cities: Bengaluru, Delhi, Gurgaon, Noida, Mumbai, and Hyderabad. Amazon launched diagnostics services in partnership with Bengaluru-based Orange Health Lab, a five-year-old company that collects samples from homes and claims to deliver test results within an hour. Some Background Amazon ventured into online medicine delivery in 2020. It currently delivers medicines ordered online through third-party sellers, unlike competitors such as 1MG, MedPlus, and Apollo, which operate their own retail medical stores. Amazon currently limits its same-day delivery service to only 23 cities. In 2018—nearly two years before COVID-19 hit—the Delhi High Court had ordered a ban on the online sale of medicines. However, during the pandemic, the central government issued notifications permitting online delivery of medicines, provided customers submitted prescriptions. Retailers must be licensed to sell medicines under Form 20 or Form 21 of the Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945. When Amazon launched its online pharmacy in 2020, the All India Organisation of Chemists & Druggists (AIOCD), which represents around 8 lakh members, wrote a letter to then-CEO Jeff Bezos, Amazon India’s head, and Prime Minister Narendra Modi. The letter stated that existing regulations do not permit the home delivery of medicines. Why it Matters: Online medicine delivery companies have increasingly entered the at-home diagnostic services market. Platforms such as MedPlus, 1MG, and hospital chains like Apollo now offer similar services. India still lacks a specific law for online pharmacies. E-commerce platforms operate under the Information Technology Act, 2000. However, medicine sales are regulated by the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940; Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945; Pharmacy Act, 1948; and Indian Medical Council Act, 1956. The Indian Medical Association says online medicine delivery violates several provisions of existing laws. It raises concerns about the licensing of sellers, the quality and standards of medicines, potential misuse of prescriptions, the authenticity of prescribers, and the absence of qualified pharmacists to handle customer inquiries. Amazon’s push into at-home diagnostic services, within an already unregulated online medicine market intensifies privacy concerns, especially as the tech giant now handles teleconsultations, diagnostic tests, and drug deliveries. These concerns deepen in light of government programs such as ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account), which aims to link all health records to a single ABHA ID number. Also Read: Chemists association opposes Amazon’s entry into e-pharmacy space Update: Flipkart and Amazon get notices from DCGI for allegedly selling adulterated cosmetics “Our Plan Is Not To Shut Down”: Pillup Founder Ankur Solanki Explores Regulations the E-Pharma Sector Needs The post Amid Legal Gray Area of Online Pharmacies, Amazon Expands into India’s At-Home Diagnostics appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Supreme Court Stays Deportation Of Sri Lankan Tamil Immigrant
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: The Supreme Court today stayed a 2019 deportation order passed against a Sri Lankan Tamil immigrant and called for the response of Indian authorities on his request for permission to physically visit Switzerland Embassy for processing of a visa on humanitarian grounds. A bench of Justices KV Viswanathan and N Kotiswar Singh heard the matter and issued notice to the respondent-authorities. Since nearly 5.5 years had elapsed since the impugned deportation order was passed, the bench was inclined to seek the authorities' response as to the current status. Briefly put, the petitioner, an inmate at Trichy Special Camp, moved the Court challenging the order of deportation passed by Government on 20.11.2019 and seeking permission to physically visit theEmbassy of Switzerland to obtain visa on humanitarian grounds. His plea seeking permission to visit Switzerland embassy was dismissed by the Madras High Court in December, 2024. As per claims, he is evading Sri Lanka due to a threat to his life as a Sri Lankan Tamil. Senior Advocate Jayanth Muth Raj appeared for the petitioner and beseeched the Court to protect him from deportation to Sri Lanka. He underlined that the petitioner has spent 9 years in India (3 in prison and 6 in the detention camp). "Don't deport me, because all my family members have been eliminated...I am not going to cause any damage to India...please don't send me to Sri Lanka. If Switzerland is giving humanitarian visa, I will move there instead of going to get killed in Sri Lanka. My brother and [...] were executed after they were caught...entire family has been eliminated...", Muth Raj urged. Insofar as the allegation of the petitioner being involved in a human trafficking case, the senior counsel informed that he was acquitted in 2019 itself. It was also submitted that the petitioner would bear the expenses of the Security Officer accompanying him (to the Embassy). When Justice Viswanathan queried as to what is the imminent threat of deportation which required the matter to be listed during partial working days, Muth Raj replied, "in the war, my father and my sister-in-law have been executed. After 2019, my brother and others have been caught and they have been executed after catching...not in a fight...". He also informed the Court about an email received from the Switzerland embassy asking the petitioner to visit in person for processing of his visa application. Case Title: BASKARAN @ MAYURAN AND ANR. Versus THE UNION OF INDIA AND ORS., Diary No. 16491-2025 Related - India Not A 'Dharamshala', Can't Host Refugees From All Over : Supreme Court Rejects Sri Lankan Tamil's Plea

articles.title: The Framework behind India’s Opaque Content Blocking Regime
articles.summary: tl;dr On 8th May, 2025, X (formerly Twitter) reported that it had received executive orders from the Indian government to block over 8000 accounts in India. In the aftermath of the arbitrary blocking, Maktoob Media was one of approximately sixteen Indian digital media outlets and creators that sought support from the Internet Freedom Foundation (‘IFF’). Working alongside several legal teams, including those led by Talha Abdul Rahman and Nakul Gandhi, IFF played a crucial role in facilitating
articles.content: tl;dr On 8th May, 2025, X (formerly Twitter) reported that it had received executive orders from the Indian government to block over 8000 accounts in India. In the aftermath of the arbitrary blocking, Maktoob Media was one of approximately sixteen Indian digital media outlets and creators that sought support from the Internet Freedom Foundation (‘IFF’). Working alongside several legal teams, including those led by Talha Abdul Rahman and Nakul Gandhi, IFF played a crucial role in facilitating legal strategy and advocacy. These coordinated efforts led to the unblocking of numerous accounts over the weekend, offering significant relief to digital-first and digital-only publishers whose operations depend on social media for visibility and content distribution. Why should you care? While the restoration is welcome, the opaque and unlawful nature of the original blocking order remains deeply concerning. Throughout this entire period, the Government of India failed to comply with the statutory procedure prescribed under the Information Technology Act, 2000 (‘IT Act 2000’) and the associated Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards for Blocking for Access of Information by Public) Rules, 2009 (‘Blocking Rules, 2009’). Legal Framework for Blocking Online Content in India Blocking under Section 69A of the IT Act 2000 can happen in two ways: Through the Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards for Blocking for Access of Information by Public) Rules, 2009 (“Blocking Rules 2009”), done by the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (“MeitY”) Through the Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (“Intermediary Rules 2021”), done by the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (“MIB”). The process under the Blocking Rules, 2009 Were the 8th May blocking orders constitutional? Blocking someone’s entire online account stops that person from speaking on the platform altogether, now and in the future. This prior restraint of one’s fundamental rights is constitutional only if principles of natural justice, such as reasonable opportunity to be heard and prior notice, are followed. However, Maktoob, like many others, received no notice of its account getting blocked. In fact, editors found out through readers instead of official notification that the account was no longer accessible in India. Unilateral State action goes against the principles of the Constitution. Supreme Court jurisprudence has evolved to tell us that Article 21 guarantees all persons a right to due process : including sub-rights like a right to be heard and a right to a reasoned order. Shreya Singhal states that in any blocking done under Section 69A of the IT Act, the originator of the speech, if ‘identifiable’, must be given a chance to be heard. As a natural corollary, one also has a right to know the reasoning in the order that is finally passed. This order must establish a rational nexus between the post in question and one of the grounds under Article 19(2). Though the same is judicially reviewable, the Government has circumvented this possibility by sending notice (and presumably the final order) only to the intermediary, i.e. X Corp., who has no incentive to challenge the action in Indian courts. Moreover, the doctrine of proportionality dictates that any restriction of a fundamental right must not be more severe than needed to achieve the ‘legitimate state aim’ in question. If the rational objective or ‘legitimate State aim’ behind the blocking is to take down an instance of speech under Section 69A, the same can be achieved via the blocking of specific posts. Preventing someone from speaking altogether is disproportionate, arbitrary and unconstitutional. What to do if it happens to you? In case your account is blocked, please first reach out to the intermediary, via their Grievance Officer as well as the Ministries themselves. The email address for the Designated Officer under the Blocking Rules 2009 is cyberlaw@meity.gov.in , and the email address for the Secretary of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting is secy.inb@nic.in . Reproduced below is a template you can follow: “Respectfully, I am the owner, originator, and editor of the X/Twitter account @ ABC (URL: https://x.com/ ABC ). [Add a brief line on who you are and how you use this account.] ABC is a ____ with Registration Number: ____, with a digital extension as a website and social media accounts. On ______ (date/time) , X/Twitter informed me that ______.. [quote the notification] To date, no government authority has contacted me, issued notice, or provided an opportunity to be heard. I have not received any reasoned order explaining the blocking. I therefore request: Immediate restoration of the account. The blocking is disproportionate, unlawful, and causing ongoing harm to my rights. A copy of the reasoned blocking order (if any), along with all supporting material and file notings. A show‑cause notice and a personal hearing so I may defend myself. Kindly respond within 48 hours. I am reachable at ____ Yours sincerely, [Name]” If you have not received a notice from the Designated Officer (‘DO’), you can serve them with a legal notice via email, stating that as per Shreya Singhal , you have a right to be notified under Rule 8. If their response indicates that the blocking was enacted under emergency provisions (Rule 9/Rule 16) and therefore there was no need to serve notice, you can respond by requesting a ‘post-decisional’ hearing. If the response, relying on the confidentiality clause, denies access to the blocking order, you can write back citing the Delhi High Court’s decision in Tanul Thakur v. Union of India , where it allowed the petitioner to access the original blocking order with a redaction of information pertaining to third parties such as the complainant. Conclusion At 4:00 PM, Maktoob Media received official confirmation from X that its account had been restored in India — just as it had finalized a petition for filing before the Supreme Court. Coordinated efforts led to the unblocking of numerous accounts over the weekend, but the actions of the Union government deserve to be scrutinised both by the judiciary and the populace.
